sentences.text: Lillian Saunders.
S-A-U-N-D-E-R-S.
Konigsbach, K-O-N-I-G-S-B-A-C-H.
Over the O.
Lily Konigsbach.
Well, they, they called me [NON-ENGLISH], Lily, Lilo, [NON-ENGLISH].
Munkacs, which was Czechoslovakia.
M-U-N-K-A-C-S.
--umlaut over the last A.
Czechoslovakia.
It changed-- in 1919, it changed to Czech.
And in 1939, it changed back to Hungary.

It's-- comes down-- whether it has been correctly said, but I will say it the way I was told, that my great-great-grandfather was given this honorary name because he had a position with the-- around the court, whatever he may have been around the court.
And they were giving people Schwartz, Weiss, and so-- whatever their features indicated.
But he was given the name Konigsbach.
And we don't have relatives [LAUGHS] over-- because there was a-- that name, that was the only one that-- there were girls.
And the names-- the name just did not get very popular.
Because he must have been in a favorite position there where they, they wanted to honor him with that special name.
And so we've been very proud of it.
And, of course, there may have been some, some alterations.
And I can't be sure that whatever I say actually happened so.
But I was told that way.
And I will repeat it.
Nathan, Nathan.
David.
In Ungvar.
U-N-G-V-A-R, umlaut on the--
That was, at that time, Austria-Hungary.
He was a leather worker.
He made pocketbooks and suitcases.
And he was a-- the finer leather worker.
He did finer work.
In Budapest.
They went to Budapest at a young age with their mother.
And they were working there.
And he had a job, and-- with a Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And he was-- he was working for them for many years.
And then got married, and went to Debrecen and opened up a suitcase factory.
I don't know how big it was.
It was in [NON-ENGLISH], so I don't know how big that was.
But he made suitcases.
And they had a nice lifestyle there.
And then he was taken into the army in 1914.
Sure.
D-E-B-R-E-C-Z-E-N.
That was--
That was Hungary.
That was Hungary.
That was motherland.
From Debrecen, he was taken into-- he was in the war.
And he stayed in the war until 1918, end of 1918.
He was in Russian camp.
And then when he returned, my mother and the two children, they had by then-- my mother returned to Szerednye.
And that's where he came after the war.
And they lived there for a year or two.
And then went to Munkacs, where they had opened the business.
And eventually, built a house.
And that's where we lived, and I was born.
S-Z-E-R-E-N-N-Y-E, Szerednye.
My brother and I-- my brother Jeno and I were born there.
Well--
Well, he was a, a very happy man.
He was-- he loved to sing.
And I remember all the songs until this day.
The songs are very, very dear to me.
And I get gooseflesh when I sing them.
And I love to sing them.
And he was just a very happy father.
And he worked.
He worked a lot.
He loved to do his craft.
And then he loved to do gardening.
We had a garden, of course.
We-- that had vegetables and flowers.
And my mother loved flowers.
And she would travel anywhere to get unusual plants and bulbs.
And they worked in the garden.
And they, they had the business.
And my father was very involved in temple with, with the rabbi, the, the infamous rabbi, who, who was harmful to the Jews by not letting them go to Palestine when they wanted to go.
And, of course--
Rabbi-- you-- I have-- I don't recall.
But on your-- on your notes, if you'll remind me, I'll-- maybe during the interview, I'll--
The Munkacs Rebbe, all right.
Very.
And very shortsighted.
And to him, going to Palestine meant you will not observe Shabbos.
And you will work on holidays, and-- the restrictions of religion.
And he condemned anyone who would dare to go to Palestine.
And his own son-in-law went against his wish.
And they cursed him openly.
So I have-- I have reservations about religion.
Yes.
[SIGHS]
My mother was a--
Her name was Szeren, S-Z-E-R-E-N, with an umlaut.
And she was one of three daughters.
Grunfeld.
G-R-U-N-F-E-L-D.
And they, they were very comfortable with land, vineyards, and a bakery.
And they were called [NON-ENGLISH] in Russian because [NON-ENGLISH] is a-- is-- they had the only house that was covered with metal roof.
So, of course, they gave them the name of metal-roof people.
And my grandfather was a manipulator.
And he knew how to make money.
And he had-- so they were very comfortable.
And my mother and her sister came to America when they were young.
And she was here for a little while.
And she worked as a, a-- now, now we call them nanny.
I'm sure it was called taking care of two children for Thomashefsky, the entertainer.
And then she was telling stories about her, her sojourns to Connecticut, which was a very strange word when we were children.
We heard that expression.
And, and yet she had a very interesting time.
And then her sister remained in America.
And she went back to Europe.
And she missed her mother very much.
They were very close.
In the United States.
And she remembered that her mother fainted at the boat.
And she just couldn't get that out of her mind.
And she returned.
She couldn't-- the memory of her mother's fainting was very clear in her mind.
And she couldn't get that out.
She couldn't forget that.
And so she came back.
And that was interesting that how my father met her because she was taking back some message from someone.
And my father has gone to Szerednye to-- to pick up that message.
And when he met her, he fell in love with her.
And they got married.
So the message--
--was good.
I can't-- they were-- they, they must have married 1912 or '11.
I had an oldest brother, who was the only talented person in the family.
He, he played the violin.
He was a violin player, [LAUGHS] and he wrote music.
And he performed.
And he was a-- he was forever working on his music.
And the house had a lot of sounds.
There was the mandolin, the violin, and the zither.
And the-- we, we enjoyed music.
And we had the kind of house that we didn't have a telephone, so the people didn't call that-- can I see you?
They just knocked on the door.
And they came in.
And they-- we were-- had food.
And we had games.
And we were reading the Yiddish books, the Sholem Aleichem and the Chaim Nachman Bialik books, these-- they were reading in the evening.
There was no television, of course.
So we were occupied on the weekends with the-- with the fun of music and reading and eating.
And I'm not going to mention the arguments there were because they are over.
But we had them, too.
[LAUGHS] But I have forgotten about any arguments.
But I just remember the, the fun things.
And we played chess.
And I was standing on the chair.
And I was playing chess, and I thought I know how.
And it was just-- it was a good, active household.
Adolf.
And he was taken into the labor camp and then into Russia, where he starved to death.
My other-- my oldest sister-- these two were born in Debrecen.
And she was just a very sweet, loving, caring person.
And she sewed.
She was a very--
Helen, H-E-L-E-N.
And she was talented in her sewing because she-- all her clothes, she would sew.
And she would make her styles according to her imagination.
She would-- she enjoyed that.
And she got married and had two children.
And she was killed with both her children-- girls-- in Auschwitz in 1944.
And her husband went to the labor camp.
And he--
--returned.
His name was Moritz.
Moritz.
M-O-R-I-C.
Rosenfeld.
He--
He, he came back, yes, and he remarried.
And he was-- I, I saw him in Prague.
He came to visit us.
And, of course, the bitterness of those days that his family didn't come back and that they were two beautiful children he had.
And his-- so it was-- it was a very, very emotional time.
But after that, he went back to a-- to somewhere in the-- in the Carpathian.
I don't know where he settled.
But I only saw him once after the war.
And then-- and I-- my brother Moric.
My-- that-- no, let me go according to the years.
My sister Reggie was born in 1920.
My father returned from the war in 1919, and she was born in 1920.
And she died [SIGHS] last year.
Her name was Reggie.
We called her-- Regina was her name here.
But we called her Reggie, R-E-G-G-I-E.
And she was-- she didn't-- she went to school.
And then she worked in the business.
She helped with the paperwork and-- because we had the pocketbook and suitcase store.
And she helped there.
And she got married and had two daughters.
And then Moric, who was-- who loved to play the accordion.
And he was a-- he is a happy-- he was.
He's now not very happy-go-lucky.
He is not well.
And he loved to play the accordion.
And he had a motorcycle.
And he covered every territory he could.
And he took on his back his accordion.
And he was making everybody happy.
And when he went to labor camp, he had this accordion with him.
And I heard so many times that they said that he kept them, their spirit up, by always playing that accordion.
And, and he is-- he has-- he was stuck in, in Munkacs.
He didn't come out till-- 20 years ago, he came out to his country.
After the war, yes.
He was in-- the camps, I won't give you the names because I may not be correct.
But in the-- in the-- he was in labor camp.
And then he was taken to, to the camps.
I mentioned-- I think he may have been-- he told me Dachau.
But I can't seem to remember.
And then I have my brother, Jeno, who was-- who was taken with us to Auschwitz because he was too young to be taken to labor camp.
And he's--
He was born in 1924.
And, and then I was born.
I was the baby.
And every one of them, till just recently, they all thought of me always as the baby, and always spoiled me and loved me.
And so I had a very-- I had a very nice family setup.
We were very loyal and loving to each other.
We were known as the most loyal family around.
Well, we were in a Czechish-- we were Czechs.
And we had the business.
And we had the-- go to school and do our lifestyle.
And it was this-- we had a very good life.
What we didn't like under the Czechs was the taxing.
They wanted an awful lot of tax.
And they got it best they could.
They would [LAUGHS] search for it.
But we didn't even know how well we had it.
My father was a Hungarian in his soul.
All he wanted is the peacetime, [HUNGARIAN].
He wanted to have it from the time that he remembered in Hungary, that it was very nice.
And we all lived for the time when we'll be reoccupied by Hungarians.
And that was living in a-- in the wrong world because the Czechs were wonderful.
They had their-- they had their little schtickle, too.
They had-- they were not-- they were not anti-Semites.
But there were people there who, who just didn't befriend the Jews all that much.
But we were onto ourselves mostly.
And life under the Czechs was good.
We had the money in Prague.
Every child had a sum put away in Prague, which we never retrieved.
We never got that.
I don't know why that we didn't go.
We couldn't go after it.
We didn't know how.
So it was lost.
And we had our growing-- the growing pains, and the survival, and, and the making-- keeping the family together.
We had a 12 o'clock lunch, midday dinner, where everybody in the family was there exactly on time.
There was no such thing as, sorry, I'm late.
That was the ritual of the family.
And we were very close.
And the store was closed from 12:00 to 2:00.
And we were around the table for that time.
And then we went back to work.
The rest of the day was any which way you wanted to.
You could eat anywhere you wanted.
So we were very close.
And my mother, the pride she had in us was nice.
I remember-- now, I remember the face or expressions that she had when she would lean out the window and look at her children going to temple or going on a holiday, somebody dressed up.
It was a mother who was proud.
She had three boys, three girls.
What more could she ask?
Mhm.
What-- I gather that you, you went to temple, so--
Yes.
There was only one kind I know of.
And that was men were downstairs, and women were upstairs.
And for me, it was just natural to go sometimes to temple with my mother.
Yes.
Oh, we had--
We had every holiday.
Every holiday was very carefully observed, with all the good food.
My mother was a very good cook.
She was one of those cooks that the food was delicate and tasty.
There wasn't much of it.
No one overate.
And there was always enough for a beggar or two to have leftovers.
But every food was-- the food there was good.
It was fresh.
And it well-made, delicately.
And it was nice food.
And we had-- my mother inherited some nice dishes and linens, and whatever.
And it was made for her somewhere.
So we had a nice table.
And we were proud of our lifestyle.
Well,
--we, the-- we-- one-- the one we went to wasn't in our section.
And there was on the other side of temple, there were also temples.
But we did not frequent that.
No, no.
We were religious.
But my mother did not like the ultra-religious behavior.
She did not-- she would have a name for those who were overdoing it.
And she called them [NON-ENGLISH].
She didn't like [NON-ENGLISH].
Yet, she, she would expect the boys, when they came to the house, to wear the hat.
And she expected that they have respect for the religion.
But she did not-- she did not like this carrying it to an extreme.
Well, my father, when he lived in Budapest, he had no religious affiliations.
He was just not religious at all.
But when he met my mother, she has brought him over to be a temple-goer.
But he went to the rabbi in-- to sit by his right hand.
And he was-- he, he enjoyed that the, the, the, the people who would get together and schmooze.
It was a fun thing to be at the rabbi's table.
So--
Well, we had-- we had always a visitor, or some salesman who was stuck in Munkacs and couldn't go back home, or someone who needed a Friday night or a Saturday meal.
So we always had one or two people at the table.
That was-- that was-- oh, that was everyone's habit there.
Anybody who had a home and was comfortable had made sure that they are charitable.
And my mother was in the-- my brother told me that.
I didn't remember it years ago.
He told me my mother was in the women's association.
Because I remember going with her to the hospitals.
And she would take food every Saturday.
She would take over to the hospitals challah and the-- whatever goodies.
And that was from the organization that they organized that.
And so she was very charitable, but in a-- charitable in the way that don't tell that you give.
Because once you told, you no longer gave charity.
Then you gave yourself a present.
So give quietly.
Don't let anyone know that-- and don't shame anybody who puts their hand out.
Because if they put their hand out, they shame themselves.
And if they must, they will do it.
And if they are comfortable and they still want it, don't shame them.
Because they have a reason for doing that.
So a charity-- she understood charity for what it was.
Well, first of all, there was the chanting of the prayers.
And, and, and the singing took place mostly between Saturday afternoon before the sun set, and the lights were not to be put on yet, and the [NON-ENGLISH] was about to be lit.
There was this two hour between this quiet and dark.
And then my father would sing in the dark.
And we would all join him.
And that was the time I remember.
And then he would sing.
When he would be working, he would also sing, but not--
Yeah.
I, I, I-- you know, when you suddenly-- you-- it comes to you.
But-- [NON-ENGLISH  SINGING].
And this was a song about a rooster who was in the forest, who has green feathers and red legs.
And it was just a very pleasant song.
And--
Well, I--
I think this would be awkward.
I don't know.
But, but I--
--I sing it often.
And, and I-- and I just-- it just brings back the memories of that--
--Shabbos afternoon.
Well--
There was the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And, and then he would read-- sing Hebrew songs.
I don't.
I don't.
But when he was singing them, they were just-- they were just pleasant.
The time went very pleasantly.
And then, then the [NON-ENGLISH] lighting.
And I was the youngest.
Therefore, I held my hand up high.
And I was holding the [NON-ENGLISH] and the, the little container for the nutmeg, the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that was held high.
And everybody smelled it.
And, and the, the prayers over that.
And after that was a bite to eat and to work.
Because they opened the store for one more day-- one more hour.
Saturday, one more hour was open for good luck or little extra, cash or whatever because Sunday, stores were not open.
The store was in the middle of town, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a fairly modern-- it was the most modern structure they had.
It was a, a-- like a, a corridor.
And it had stores in it.
It may have had 50 stores or more on each side of-- each side, they may have had 20, 25, 26 stores on each side.
And from-- everything that you wanted, you could get there, linens and fabrics and hats and bags, and you name it.
And then there was this-- on the other end, there was this very famous shoe store, [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was a big corner, a big store.
And it was-- it was a nice structure.
And then there was the central, the [NON-ENGLISH].
And it, it was just a very pleasant place.
The store-- we had a front-- we had suitcases lined up on one wall.
And the other wall were glass shelves where we had pocketbooks.
And I-- now I call it the carriage tray because you had to be very comfortable to buy there.
And so we had the-- all kinds of purses and, and bags.
And if you needed a holster for a gun, my father would make it.
And in the back, my father did the repairs.
Or whoever ordered a suitcase, he did that.
And he loved to be there.
He loved to work late.
And my grandmother would come to the back door and bring him another snack, too.
That was where he-- her way of pampering and remembering that he is her baby.
No, there--
--was just a stove where they were warming the, the glue.
The glue had to be kept warm.
It smelled.
But we, we didn't know that it was bad.
It was good.
For us--
--was good.
--leather-- the smell of leather must have been--
Leather, yes.
--still love that smell of leather--
--yes.
